topic: National Symbols, Emblems & State Symbols of India

questions.text: The motto 'Satyameva Jayate' inscribed on India's national emblem is taken from:
questions.explanation: Satyameva Jayate (Truth Alone Triumphs) is taken from the Mundaka Upanishad, one of the principal Upanishads. It is written below the national emblem in Devanagari script. The emblem itself is derived from the Lion Capital of Ashoka at Sarnath.

questions.text: India's national flag was designed by:
questions.explanation: Pingali Venkaiah (from Bhattlapenumarru, Andhra Pradesh) designed India's national flag. He also designed an earlier version of the flag for the Indian National Congress. The Constituent Assembly formally adopted the current flag design on July 22, 1947.

questions.text: The national animal of India (Bengal Tiger) replaced which animal in 1973?
questions.explanation: The Bengal Tiger replaced the Lion as India's national animal in 1973, the year Project Tiger was launched to protect the dwindling tiger population. The lion remains associated with India's national emblem (Lion Capital of Ashoka).

questions.text: The Indian national flag must be made of which material?
questions.explanation: By law (Bureau of Indian Standards), India's national flag must be made only of Khadi (hand-spun and hand-woven fabric). The Flag Code of India 2002 specifies that the flag shall be made of hand-spun and hand-woven wool, cotton, or silk Khadi bunting.

questions.text: On which date was India's national flag officially adopted?
questions.explanation: The national flag was adopted by the Constituent Assembly on July 22, 1947 — NOT on Independence Day (August 15) and NOT on Republic Day (January 26). This is the most common wrong answer in SSC exams on this topic.

questions.text: How many spokes does the Ashoka Chakra in India's national flag have?
questions.explanation: The Ashoka Chakra in India's national flag has 24 spokes, representing the 24 hours in a day — symbolising constant progress. The chakra is in navy blue colour in the white band of the flag. It represents the Dhamma Chakra (Wheel of Righteousness) from Buddhism.

The Ganges River Dolphin (Platanista gangetica), also known as Susu, was declared India's national aquatic animal in 2009. It is found in the Ganga, Brahmaputra, and Meghna river systems. It is blind and uses echolocation.

questions.text: The 'Jana Gana Mana' was first publicly performed at which Congress session?
questions.explanation: Jana Gana Mana was first performed on December 27, 1911 at the Calcutta (Kolkata) session of the Indian National Congress. Rabindranath Tagore composed it. The song was adopted as the national anthem by the Constituent Assembly on January 24, 1950.

questions.text: India's national currency symbol ₹ was designed by whom?
questions.explanation: The Indian Rupee symbol ₹ was designed by D. Udaya Kumar, a postgraduate design student from IIT Bombay. The symbol is derived from the Devanagari 'Ra' combined with the Latin 'R,' with double horizontal lines representing equality. It was adopted on July 15, 2010.

questions.text: Vande Mataram, India's national song, was first publicly sung at the 1896 Congress session by:
questions.explanation: Vande Mataram was first publicly performed at the 1896 Calcutta Congress session by Rabindranath Tagore (through his school, some accounts differ on who performed it exactly). Bankim Chandra Chattopadhyay composed it (in his novel Anand Math, 1882).

The Indian Elephant (Elephas maximus indicus) was declared India's national heritage animal in 2010. This is different from the national animal (Bengal Tiger). The elephant was given heritage status to provide it greater protection under Project Elephant.

questions.text: India adopted the Saka Calendar as its national calendar in which year?
questions.explanation: The Saka Calendar (also called the Indian National Calendar) was adopted as India's official civil calendar on March 22, 1957, based on the recommendations of the Calendar Reform Committee (1955) chaired by Meghnad Saha. Chaitra is its first month.

questions.text: The national emblem of India is derived from the Lion Capital found at:
questions.explanation: India's national emblem is adapted from the Lion Capital of Ashoka found at Sarnath, Uttar Pradesh (near Varanasi). The capital was erected by Emperor Ashoka in approximately 250 BCE and is now in the Sarnath Museum.

questions.text: India's national flower is the Lotus. What is the scientific name?
questions.explanation: Nelumbo nucifera is the scientific name of the Sacred Lotus (Kamal), India's national flower. It is revered in Hinduism and Buddhism. The lotus grows in muddy water while remaining clean — symbolising purity. Nymphaea is the water lily (different plant).

questions.text: India's national river Ganga was declared so in which year?
questions.explanation: The Ganga was declared India's National River on November 4, 2008 by the Prime Minister Manmohan Singh. The National Ganga River Basin Authority was also established that day under the Environment Protection Act to clean and conserve the river.

questions.text: Gujarat's state animal is the Asiatic Lion. Where in India can wild Asiatic Lions be found?
questions.explanation: Wild Asiatic Lions (Panthera leo persica) are found ONLY in the Gir National Park and Wildlife Sanctuary in Gujarat, India. This is the last wild habitat of Asiatic Lions. India's national animal is the Bengal Tiger, but Gujarat's state animal is the Asiatic Lion.
